6790.text: On hearing an appeal from a decision of the Court of First Instance, the Court of Appeal may:

6792.text: Appeals To The Court Of First Instance

6794.text: Unless another Rule, Practice Direction or enactment provides otherwise, on hearing an appeal, the Court of First Instance may:

6798.text: Every appeal will be limited to a review of the decision of the lower Court unless:

6799.text: (1) the Court considers that in the circumstances of an individual appeal it would be in the interests of justice to hold a re-hearing;

6801.text: (3) any other Rule or enactment requires a re-hearing.

6803.text: The hearing of an appeal will be a re-hearing (as opposed to a review of the decision of the lower Court) if the appeal is from the decision of a person or other body and the person or other body:

6804.text: (1) did not hold a hearing to come to that decision; or

6805.text: (2) held a hearing to come to that decision, but the procedure adopted did not provide for the consideration of evidence.

6807.text: The appeal Court may exercise its powers in relation to the whole or part of an order of the lower Court.

6809.text: At the hearing of the appeal a party may not rely on a matter not contained in his appeal notice unless the appeal Court gives permission.

6812.text: Unless it orders otherwise, the appeal Court will not receive:

6813.text: (1) oral evidence; or (2) evidence which was not before the lower Court.

6815.text: Subject to Rule 44.114, the Court may receive further evidence, including:

6818.text: The appeal Court may draw any inference of fact which it considers justified on the evidence.

6821.text: The Court of Appeal will allow an appeal from a decision of the Court of First Instance where the decision of the lower Court was:

6822.text: (1) wrong; or (2) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings in the lower Court.

6824.text: The Court of First Instance will allow an appeal from a decision of a tribunal provided for in the Law, DIFC Law or Rules of Court where the decision was:

6825.text: (1) wrong in relation to a question of law; (2) unjust because of procedural unfairness or a miscarriage of justice; and/or (3) wrong in relation to any other matter provided for in or under DIFC Law.

6827.text: The Court of First Instance will allow an appeal not referred to in Rule 44.118 where the decision was:

6828.text: (1) wrong; or (2) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings.

6831.text: The fact that a Part 32 offer or payment into Court has been made must not be disclosed to any Judge of the appeal Court who is to hear or determine:

6832.text: (1) an application for permission to appeal; or

6834.text: until all questions (other than costs) have been determined.

6836.text: Rule 44.120 does not apply if the Part 32 offer or payment into Court is relevant to the substance of the appeal.

6838.text: Rule 44.120 does not prevent disclosure in any application in the appeal proceedings if disclosure of the fact that a Part 32 offer or payment into Court has been made is properly relevant to the matter to be decided.

6839.text: Who May Exercise The Powers Of The Court Of Appeal

6841.text: The Registrar may exercise the jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal with the consent of the Chief Justice to do all things necessary or convenient for the purpose of assisting the Judges of the Court of Appeal in the exercise of their powers or duties.

6843.text: A single Judge may exercise the appellate jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal relating to an application:

6844.text: (1) for permission to appeal to the Court of Appeal (and to grant such permission subject to such conditions as may be ordered by the single judge);

6845.text: (2) for an extension or reduction of time for any document to be filed in an appeal to the Court of Appeal (including a general power to amend the times fixed by the rules or by order of the court, as may be the case);

6846.text: (3) for leave to amend the grounds of appeal;

6848.text: (5) to strike out an appeal in the event that:

6849.text: (a) the appellant does not file a document within the time ordered and notice has been given by the Court in the order for filing that the appeal will be struck out if filing is not made strictly on time; or

6850.text: (b) the appellant does not attend the hearing of the appeal in person or by his Counsel on the date fixed for hearing; or

6851.text: (6) to strike out an appeal in the event that a skeleton is not submitted.

6852.text: (7) The single judge shall also have the power to decide any matter of case management relating to the appeal on behalf of the full bench of the Court of Appeal.

6854.text: Decisions of the Registrar or a single Judge and under Rules 44.123 or 11.124 may be made without an oral hearing.

6856.text: A party may request any decision of the Registrar or the single Judge under Rules 44.123 or 44.124 (3) or (4) made without a hearing to be reviewed by the Court of Appeal at an oral hearing.

6858.text: A request under Rule 44.126 must be filed within 7 days after the party is served with notice of the decision.

6860.text: A single Judge may refer any matter for a decision by a Court consisting of two or more judges .

6861.text: Dismissal Of Applications Or Appeals By Consent

6863.text: Where an appellant does not wish to pursue an application or an appeal, he may request the appeal

6864.text: Court for an order that his application or appeal be dismissed. If such a request is granted it will usually be on the basis that the appellant pays the costs of the application or appeal.

6866.text: If the appellant wishes to have the application or appeal dismissed without costs, his request must be accompanied by a consent signed by the respondent or his legal representative stating that the respondent consents to the dismissal of the application or appeal without costs.

6868.text: Where a settlement has been reached disposing of the application or appeal, the parties may make a joint request to the Court asking that the application or appeal be dismissed by consent. If the request is granted the application or appeal will be dismissed.

6869.text: Allowing Unopposed Appeals Or Applications Without A Hearing

6871.text: The appeal Court will not normally make an order allowing an appeal unless satisfied that the decision of the lower Court was wrong, but the appeal Court may set aside or vary the order of the lower Court with consent and without determining the merits of the appeal, if it is satisfied that there are good and sufficient reasons for doing so. Where the appeal Court is requested by all parties to allow an application or an appeal the Court may consider the request without a hearing. The request should set out the relevant history of the proceedings and the matters relied on as justifying the proposed order and be accompanied by a copy of the proposed order.

6872.text: Availability Of Reserved Judgments Before Hand Down

6874.text: Rules 44.134 and 44.135 apply where the presiding Judge is satisfied that the result of the appeal will attract no special degree of confidentiality or sensitivity.

6876.text: A copy of the written judgment may be made available to the parties’ legal representatives by 4 p.m. two clear working days before judgment is due to be pronounced or such other period as the Court may direct. This can be shown, in confidence, to the parties but only for the purpose of obtaining instructions and on the strict understanding that the judgment, or its effect, is not to be disclosed to any other person. A working day is any day on which the Court Office is open for business.

6878.text: The appeal will be listed for judgment and the judgment handed down at the appropriate time.

6879.text: Attendance Of Legal Representatives On The Handing Down Of A Reserved

6882.text: Where any consequential orders are agreed, the parties’ legal representatives need not attend on the handing down of a reserved judgment. Where a legal representative does attend the Court may, if it considers such attendance unnecessary, disallow the costs of the attendance. If the parties do not indicate that they intend to attend, where the appeal was heard by more than one Judge, the judgment may be handed down by a single member of the Court.

6885.text: The parties must, in respect of any draft agreed orders file 4 copies in the Court Office, no later than 12 noon on the working day before the judgment is handed down.

6887.text: A copy of a draft order must bear the case reference, the date the judgment is to be handed down and the name of the presiding Judge.

6890.text: Any proposed correction to the draft judgment should be sent to the Registry with a copy to any other party.

6893.text: Costs are likely to be assessed by way of immediate assessment at the following hearings:

6896.text: Parties attending any of the hearings referred to in Rule 44.140 should be prepared to deal with the immediate assessment.

6897.text: Appeals To The Court Of First Instance From The Director Of Employment Standards

6899.text: Rules 44.143 to 44.152 apply to appeals to the Court of First Instance from a determination, decision, or fine of the Director of Employment Standards pursuant to Article 83(1) of the Employment Law, DIFC Law 4 of 2005.

6901.text: An appeal referred to in Rule 44.142 shall be filed within 30 days of receipt of the determination, decision, or fine appealed and in accordance with the requirements of this Part.

6903.text: The Director of Employment Standards must be named as a party to an appeal referred to in Rule 44.142.

6905.text: The Court may grant a stay of the decision appealed from until the disposition of the appeal.

6907.text: The Court may attach conditions to the stay, including requiring a party to deposit as security part or all of a monetary order.

6909.text: The Director of Employment Standards shall provide the Court with the record that was before him at the time the determination, order or fine was made, including any witness statement and documents considered by him.

6911.text: The Court may consider any other relevant evidence, in addition to the record.

6913.text: Before considering the appeal, the Court of First Instance may:

6914.text: (1) refer the matter back to the Director of Employment Standards for further investigation; or (2) recommend that an attempt be made to settle the matter.

6916.text: The Court may dismiss the appeal without a hearing if satisfied that:

6917.text:
(1) the appeal is not within the Court’s jurisdiction;   
(2) the appeal is frivolous or trivial or is not brought in good faith.

6919.text: On hearing the appeal the Court may:

6922.text: The Director of Employment Standards shall comply with any directions the Court gives to him.

6924.text: 44.153 No appeal lies from a decision of the Court of First Instance or the Court of Appeal on an appeal.

6927.text: The Court of Appeal or the Court of First Instance will not reopen a final determination of any appeal unless:

6928.text:
(1) it is necessary to do so in order to avoid real injustice;   
(2) the circumstances are exceptional and make it appropriate to reopen the appeal; and (3) there is no alternative effective remedy.

6930.text: In Rules 44.154, 44.156, 44.159 and 44.163, “appeal” includes an application for permission to appeal.

6932.text: Permission is needed to make an application under Rule 44.154 to reopen a final determination of an appeal even in cases where permission was not needed for the original appeal.

6934.text: Permission to reopen a final determination of an appeal must be sought from the Court whose decision the applicant wishes to reopen.

6936.text: The application for permission must be made by application notice and supported by written evidence, verified by a statement of truth.

6938.text: A copy of the application for permission must not be served on any other party to the original appeal unless the Court so directs.

6940.text: Where the Court directs that the application for permission is to be served on another party, that party may within 14 days of the service on him of the copy of the application file and serve a written statement either supporting or opposing the application.

6942.text: The application for permission, and any written statements supporting or opposing it, will be considered on paper by a single Judge.

6944.text: There is no right to an oral hearing of an application for permission unless, exceptionally, the Judge so directs.

6946.text: The Judge will not grant permission without directing the application to be served on the other party to the original appeal and giving him an opportunity to make representations.

6948.text: There is no right of appeal or review from the decision of the Judge on the application for permission, which is final.

6951.text: THESE RULES WILL APPLY TO JUDGMENTS AND ORDERS ISSUED BEFORE 25 MAY 2017.

6952.text: Scope Of This Part And Interpretation

6954.text: The Rules in this Part apply to appeals to:

6955.text: (1) the Court of Appeal; and (2) the Court of First Instance.

6960.text: This Part is subject to any Rule, enactment or Practice Direction which sets out special provisions with regard to any particular category of appeal.

6963.text: Unless the appeal CourtG or the lower CourtG orders otherwise, an appeal shall not operate as a stayG of any order or decision of the lower CourtG .

6966.text: An appellantG or respondentG requires permission to appeal:

6967.text: (1) where the appeal is to the Court of Appeal, except where the appeal is against a committal order; (2) where the appeal is to the Court of First Instance, except where the appeal is against: (a) a refusal by the Registrar of Companies to grant authorisation to transfer incorporation under Article 122(1) of the Companies Law (Amended and Restated), DIFC Law 3 of 2006; or

6968.text: (b) a refusal by the Registrar of Companies to grant authorisation to transfer a Limited Partnership under Article 63(1) of the Limited Partnership Law, DIFC Law 4 of 2006.

6970.text: An application for permission to appeal may be made:

6971.text: (1) to the lower CourtG at the hearing at which the decision to be appealed was made; or (2) to the appeal CourtG in an appeal noticeG .

6973.text: An application to the appeal Court for permission to appeal under RDC 44.71(2) may not be heard by the judge against whose decision permission to appeal is sought.

6975.text: Permission to appeal may be given only where:

6976.text: (1) the CourtG considers that the appeal would have a real prospect of success; or (2) there is some other compelling reason why the appeal should be heard.

6978.text: Where a party applies for permission to appeal against a decision at the hearing at which the decision was made, the JudgeG making the decision shall state:

6979.text:
(1) whether or not the judgmentG or order is final;   
(2) whether an appeal lies from the judgmentG or order; and   
(3) whether the CourtG gives permission to appeal; and   
(4) if not, the appropriate appeal CourtG to which any further application for permission may be made.

6981.text: Where no application for permission to appeal has been made in accordance with Rule 44.6(1) but a party requests further time to make such an application, the CourtG may adjourn the hearing to give that party the opportunity to do so.

6983.text: Where the lower CourtG refuses an application for permission to appeal, a further application for permission to appeal may be made to the appeal CourtG .

6984.text: Consideration Of Permission Without A Hearing

6986.text: Applications for permission to appeal may be considered by the appeal CourtG without a hearing.

6988.text: If permission is granted without a hearing the parties will be notified of that decision and the procedure in Rules 44.93 to 44.97 will then apply.

6989.text: 44.14 If permission is refused without a hearing the parties will be notified of that decision with the reasons for it.

6991.text: Where the appeal CourtG , without a hearing, refuses permission to appeal, the person seeking permission may request the decision to be reconsidered at a hearing. This may be before the same JudgeG .

6993.text: Where the appeal CourtG refuses permission to appeal without a hearing, it may, if it considers that the application is totally without merit, make an order that the person seeking permission may not request the decision to be reconsidered at a hearing.

6995.text: A request for the decision to be reconsidered at an oral hearing must be filed at the appeal CourtG within 7 days after serviceG of the notice that permission has been refused. A copy of the request must be served by the appellantG on the respondentG at the same time.

6998.text: Rules 44.19 to 44.21 apply where an appellantG makes a request for a decision to be reconsidered at an oral hearing.

7000.text: The appellant $\mathsf { S } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ legal representativeG must, at least 4 days before the hearing, in a brief written statement:

7001.text: (1) inform the CourtG and the respondentG of the points which he proposes to raise at the hearing; and (2) set out his reasons why permission should be granted notwithstanding the reasons given for the refusal of permission.

7003.text: Notice of a permission hearing will be given to the respondentG but he is not required to attend unless the CourtG requests him to do so.

7005.text: If the CourtG requests the respondent $\mathsf { S } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ attendance at the permission hearing, the appellantG must supply the respondentG with a copy of the appeal bundle within 7 days of being notified of the request, or such other period as the CourtG may direct. The costs of providing that bundle shall be borne by the appellantG initially, but will form part of the costs of the permission application.

7009.text: (1) limit the issues to be heard; and (2) be made subject to conditions.

7011.text: Where a CourtG under Rule 44.22 gives permission to appeal on some issues only, it will:

7012.text:
(1) refuse permission on any remaining issues; or   
(2) reserve the question of permission to appeal on any remaining issues to the CourtG hearing the appeal.

7014.text: If the CourtG reserves the question of permission under Rule 44.23(2), the appellantG must, within 14 days after serviceG of the Court’sG order, inform the appeal CourtG and the respondentG in writing whether he intends to pursue the reserved issues. If the appellantG does intend to pursue the reserved issues, the parties must include in any time estimate for the appeal hearing, their time estimate for the reserved issues.

7016.text: If the appeal CourtG refuses permission to appeal on the remaining issues without a hearing and the applicant wishes to have that decision reconsidered at an oral hearing, the time limit in Rule 44.17 shall

7017.text: apply. Any application for an extension of this time limit should be made promptly. The CourtG hearing the appeal on the issues for which permission has been granted will not normally grant, at the appeal hearing, an application to extend the time limit in Rule 44.17 for the remaining issues.

7019.text: If the appeal CourtG refuses permission to appeal on remaining issues at or after an oral hearing, the application for permission to appeal on those issues cannot be renewed at the appeal hearing.

7022.text: In most cases, applications for permission to appeal will be determined without the CourtG requesting:

7023.text: (1) submissions from; or (2) if there is an oral hearing, attendance by; the respondentG .

7025.text: Where the CourtG does not request submissions from or attendance by the respondentG , costs will not normally be allowed to a respondentG who volunteers submissions or attendance.

7028.text: (1) submissions from; or (2) attendance by the respondentG ;

7029.text: the CourtG will normally allow the respondentG his costs if permission is refused.

7032.text: Case management decisions include decisions made under Rule 4.2 and decisions about:

7035.text: Where the application is for permission to appeal from a case management decision, the CourtG dealing with the application may take into account whether:

7036.text:
(1) the issue is of sufficient significance to justify the costs of an appeal;   
(2) the procedural consequences of an appeal (e.g. loss of trial date) outweigh the significance of the case management decision; and

7037.text: (3) it would be more convenient to determine the issue at or after trial.

7040.text: An appellant $\mathsf { \mathsf { S } } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice must be filed and served in all cases.

7042.text: Where the appellantG seeks permission from the appeal CourtG it must be requested in the appellant’sG notice.

7044.text: The appellant $\mathsf { \mathsf { S } } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice must set out the grounds of appeal relied on.

7047.text:
(1) set out clearly the reasons why it is said the decision of the lower CourtG was: (a) wrong; or (b) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings in the lower CourtG ; and   
(2) specify, in respect of each ground, whether the ground raises an appeal on a point of law or is an   
appeal against a finding of fact.

7048.text: Filing And Service Of Appellant’S Notice

7050.text: The appellantG must file the appellant $\cdot \mathsf { s } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice at the appeal CourtG within:

7051.text: (1) such period as may be directed by the lower CourtG which should not normally exceed 28 days and which may be longer or shorter than the period referred to in (2); or

7052.text: (2) where the CourtG makes no such direction, 14 days after the date of the decision of the lower CourtG that the appellantG wishes to appeal.

7054.text: Where the lower CourtG JudgeG announces his decision and reserves the reasons for his judgmentG or order until a later date, he should, in the exercise of powers under Rule 44.36(1), fix a period for filingG the appellant’sG notice at the appeal CourtG that takes this into account.

7056.text: Subject to Rule 44.39 and unless the appeal CourtG orders otherwise, a sealed copy of the appellant’sG notice, together with any skeleton arguments, must be served on each respondentG :

7060.text: Where the requirement to serve a skeleton argument is modified by Rule 44.75 the skeleton argument should be served as soon as it is filed.

7062.text: The appellantG must, as soon as practicable, file a certificate of serviceG of the documents referred to in Rule 44.38.

7064.text: Where the time for filingG an appellant’sG notice has expired, the appellantG must:

7065.text: (1) file the appellant’sG notice; and (2) include in that appellant $\cdot \mathsf { s } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice an application for an extension of time.

7067.text: The appellant $\mathsf { \mathsf { S } } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice should state the reason for the delay and the steps taken prior to the application being made.

7069.text: Where the appellantG is applying for permission to appeal in his appellant $\mathsf { S } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice, he must serve on the respondentsG his appellant’sG notice and skeleton argument (but not the appeal bundle), unless the appeal CourtG directs otherwise.

7072.text: (1) has been given by the lower CourtG ; or

7074.text: the appellantG must serve the appeal bundle on the respondentsG with the appellant’sG notice.

7076.text: Where the appellant $\mathsf { S } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice includes an application for an extension of time and permission to appeal has been given or is not required the respondentG has the right to be heard on that application. He must be served with a copy of the appeal bundle. However, a respondentG who unreasonably opposes an extension of time runs the risk of being ordered to pay the appellant’sG costs of that application.

7078.text: If an extension of time is given following such an application the procedure at Rules 44.93 to 44.97 applies.

7080.text: The CourtG may dispense with the requirement for serviceG of the notice on a respondentG . Any application noticeG seeking an order under Rule 9.32 to dispense with serviceG should set out the reasons relied on and be verified by a statement of truth.

7082.text: Unless the CourtG otherwise directs a respondentG need not take any action when served with an appellant’sG notice until such time as notification is given to him that permission to appeal has been given.

7085.text: An application to vary the time limit for filingG an appeal noticeG must be made to the appeal CourtG .

7087.text: The parties may not agree to extend any date or time limit set by:

7091.text: Notice of an application to be made to the appeal CourtG for a remedy incidental to the appeal may be included in the appeal noticeG or in a Part 23 application noticeG .

7093.text: The applicant must file the following documents with the notice:

7094.text: (1) one additional copy of the application noticeG for the appeal CourtG and one copy for each of the

7096.text: (2) where applicable a sealed copy of the order which is the subject of the main appeal; and

7097.text: (3) a bundle of documents in support which should include:

7099.text: (b) any witness statements and affidavitsG filed in support of the application noticeG .

7102.text: The appellantG must file the following documents together with an appeal bundle with his appellant’sG notice:

7103.text: (1) two additional copies of the appellant $\cdot \mathsf { s } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice for the appeal CourtG ; (2) one copy of the appellant’sG notice for each of the respondentsG ; and (3) one copy of his skeleton argument for each copy of the appellant’sG notice that is filed.

7105.text: An appellantG must include in his appeal bundle the following documents:

7106.text: (1) a sealed copy of the appellant’sG notice;

7107.text: (2) a sealed copy of the order being appealed;

7108.text: (3) a copy of any order giving or refusing permission to appeal, together with a copy of the Judge’sG reasons for allowing or refusing permission to appeal;

7109.text: (4) any affidavitG or witness statement filed in support of any application included in the appellant’sG notice;

7110.text: (5) a copy of his skeleton argument;

7111.text: (6) a transcript or note of judgmentG , and in cases where permission to appeal was given by the lower CourtG or is not required those parts of any transcript of evidence which are directly relevant to any question at issue on the appeal;

7112.text: (7) the claim form and statements of case (where relevant to the subject of the appeal);

7113.text: (8) any application noticeG (or case management documentation) relevant to the subject of the appeal;

7114.text: (9) in the case of judicial review or a statutory appeal, the original decision which was the subject of the

7116.text: (10) in cases where the appeal is from a tribunalG , a copy of the tribunal’sG reasons for the decision, a copy of the decision reviewed by the tribunalG and the reasons for the original decision and any document filed with the tribunalG setting out the grounds of appeal from that decision;

7117.text: (11) any other documents which the appellantG reasonably considers necessary to enable the appeal CourtG to reach its decision on the hearing of the application or appeal; and

7118.text: (12) such other documents as the CourtG may direct.

7120.text: All documents that are extraneous to the issues to be considered on the application or the appeal must be excluded. The appeal bundle may include affidavitsG , witness statements, summaries, experts’ reports and exhibits but only where these are directly relevant to the subject matter of the appeal.

7122.text: The appeal bundle must contain a certificate signed by the appellant’sG legal representativesG to the effect that they have read and understood Rule 44.55 above and that the composition of the appeal bundle complies with it.

7124.text: Where it is not possible to file all the above documents, the appellantG must indicate which documents have not yet been filed and the reasons why they are not currently available. The appellantG must then provide a reasonable estimate of when the missing document or documents can be filed and file them as soon as reasonably practicable.

7127.text: In cases where the appeal bundle comprises more than 500 pages, exclusive of transcripts, the appellant’sG legal representativesG must, after consultation with the respondent’sG legal representativesG , also prepare and file with the CourtG , in addition to copies of the appeal bundle (as amended in accordance with Rule 44.119) the requisite number of copies of a core bundle.

7129.text: The core bundle must be filed within 28 days of receipt of the order giving permission to appeal or, where permission to appeal was granted by the lower CourtG or is not required, within 28 days of the date of serviceG of the appellant’sG notice on the respondentG .

7132.text: (1) must contain the documents which are central to the appeal; and

7136.text: Rules 44.62 to 44.73 apply to the preparation of appeal bundles, supplemental respondents’G bundles where the parties are unable to agree amendments to the appeal bundle, and core bundles.

7139.text: Where documents are copied unnecessarily or bundled incompletely, costs may be disallowed. Where the provisions of this Part as to the preparation or delivery of bundles are not followed the bundle may be

7140.text: rejected by the CourtG or be made the subject of a special costs order.

7143.text: No more than one copy of any document should be included unless there is a good reason for doing otherwise (such as the use of a separate core bundle in Rule 44.58).

7146.text: The following rules regarding pagination shall apply:

7147.text: (1) Bundles must be paginated, each page being numbered individually and consecutively. The pagination used at trial must also be indicated. Letters and other documents should normally be included in chronological order. (An exception to consecutive page numbering arises in the case of core bundles where it may be preferable to retain the original numbering).

7148.text: (2) Page numbers should be inserted in bold figures at the bottom of the page and in a form that can be clearly distinguished from any other pagination on the document.

7151.text: The following rules regarding format and presentation shall apply:

7152.text: (1) Where possible the documents should be in A4 format. Where a document has to be read across rather than down the page, it should be so placed in the bundle as to ensure that the text starts nearest the spine.

7153.text: (2) Where any marking or writing in colour on a document is important, the document must be copied in colour or marked up correctly in colour.

7154.text: (3) Documents which are not easily legible should be transcribed and the transcription marked and placed adjacent to the document transcribed.

7155.text: (4) Documents in a language other than English should be translated and the translation marked and placed adjacent to the document translated. The translation should be agreed or, if it cannot be agreed, each party’s proposed translation should be included.

7156.text: (5) The size of any bundle should be tailored to its contents. A large lever arch file should not be used for just a few pages nor should files be overloaded.

7157.text: (6) Where it will assist the CourtG , different sections of the file may be separated by cardboard or other tabbed dividers so long as these are clearly indexed. Where, for example, a document is awaited when the appeal bundle is filed, a single sheet of paper can be inserted after a divider, indicating the nature of the document awaited. For example, ‘Transcript of evidence of Mr J Smith (to follow)’.

7160.text: The following rules regarding binding shall apply:

7161.text: (1) All documents, with the exception of transcripts, must be bound together. This may be in a lever arch file, ring binder or plastic folder. Plastic sleeves containing loose documents must not be used. Binders and files must be strong enough to withstand heavy use.

7162.text: (2) Large documents such as plans should be placed in an easily accessible file. Large documents which will need to be opened up frequently should be inserted in a file larger than A4 size.

7165.text: The following rules regarding indices and labels shall apply:

7166.text: (1) An index must be included at the front of the bundle listing all the documents and providing the page references for each. In the case of documents such as letters, invoices or bank statements, they may be given a general description.

7167.text: (2) Where the bundles consist of more than one file, an index to all the files should be included in the first file and an index included for each file. Indices should, if possible, be on a single sheet. The full name of the case should not be inserted on the index if this would waste space. Documents should be identified briefly but properly.

7170.text: The following rules regarding identification shall apply:

7171.text: (1) Every bundle must be clearly identified, on the spine and on the front cover, with the name of the case and the Court $' \mathsf { S } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ reference. Where the bundle consists of more than one file, each file must be numbered on the spine, the front cover and the inside of the front cover.

7172.text: (2) Outer labels should use large lettering e.g. ‘Appeal Bundle A’ or ‘Core Bundle’. The full title of the appeal and legal representatives’G names and addresses should be omitted. A label should be used on the front as well as on the spine.

7175.text: All staples, heavy metal clips, etc., must be removed.

7178.text: The following rules regarding statements of case shall apply:

7179.text: (1) Statements of case should be assembled in ‘chapter’ form — i.e. claim followed by particulars of claim, followed by further information, irrespective of date.

7180.text: (2) Redundant documents, e.g. particulars of claim overtaken by amendments, requests for further information recited in the answers given, should generally be excluded.

7183.text: The following rules regarding new documents shall apply:

7184.text: (1) Before a new document is introduced into bundles which have already been delivered to the CourtG , steps should be taken to ensure that it carries an appropriate bundle/page number so that it can be added to the CourtG documents. It should not be stapled and it should be prepared with punch holes for immediate inclusion in the binders in use.

7185.text: (2) If it is expected that a large number of miscellaneous new documents will from time to time be introduced, there should be a special tabbed empty loose-leaf file for that purpose. An index should be produced for this file, updated as necessary.

7188.text: Since correspondence between legal representativesG is unlikely to be required for the purposes of an appeal, only those letters which will need to be referred to should be copied.
